network.overall_strategy: This network prioritizes the literature-supported cyanosulfidic synthesis of an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor from HCN, acetylene, NH3, H2S, and cyanamide under Cu photoredox/UV conditions in surface geothermal settings, then converts that precursor to free arginine by hydrolysis. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively and only where supported by the supplied research: vents provide H2, reduced carbon species, pyruvate, and alanine under Fe-S mineral catalysis, supplying connected feedstocks and environmental interconnection without overclaiming direct arginine assembly at high temperature. A secondary, explicitly downgraded route uses literature-supported post-synthetic guanidination of ornithine residues in peptides by cyanamide, followed by peptide hydrolysis to release arginine; this branch is included as a plausible systems-chemistry entry consistent with the reported ornithine-to-arginine transition but is not treated as the primary route. Ten distinct pathways are enumerated as different valid traversals through the connected network, with HCN, cyanamide, ornithine-containing peptide, arginine alpha-aminonitrile, and arginine as hub/convergence species.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions; anoxic; H2-rich serpentinization setting

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated CO2 hydrogenation on Fe-S phases gives reduced one-carbon product formate.
reactions.reasoning: Supported by hydrothermal vent Fe-S catalysis literature summarized in the report, where greigite and related minerals mediate CO2 reduction under alkaline vent conditions; this step provides connected reduced-carbon feedstock without asserting unsupported direct arginine synthesis (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Russell & Martin, 2004; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from reduced carbon

conditions.other: subseafloor to alkaline vent setting; anoxic; connected to serpentinization-derived H2

reactions.mechanism: Successive mineral-catalyzed carbon fixation and reduction assemble a three-carbon alpha-keto acid pool represented by pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly notes pyruvate as plausibly formed at vents and central to downstream amino-acid chemistry. This network therefore uses pyruvate as a hydrothermal hub but keeps the step generalized and conservative rather than over-specified beyond the research summary (Cody et al., 2000; Muchowska et al., 2019; Preiner et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine

conditions.other: anoxic vent interface; mineral-surface reductive amination

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes mineral-assisted imine formation with ammonia followed by reduction to alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied report specifically identifies pyruvate reactivity and reductive amination to amino acids under vent conditions as experimentally supported. This creates a hydrothermal-to-biochemical connection without relying on unsupported ornithine synthesis (Barge et al., 2019; Muchowska et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Surface Cu-photoredox coupling of acetylene and HCN to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I) photoredox with mineral-supported surface setting
conditions.other: UV-irradiated shallow geothermal or intertidal setting; anoxic; Cu(I) required per literature

reactions.mechanism: Cu(I)-mediated C-C cross-coupling of acetylene with HCN forms acrylonitrile; mineral surfaces act as concentrating/photochemical supports.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first committed arginine-specific step in the literature-supported cyanosulfidic route. The report explicitly identifies Cu(I)-catalyzed coupling of acetylene and HCN to acrylonitrile as the entry to the three-carbon tether (Patel et al., 2015). TiO2 and sulfide minerals are included as required surface mineral agents and as plausible supports in UV-exposed sulfidic settings, but Cu(I) remains the decisive catalytic species.

reactions.name: Surface ammonia addition to acrylonitrile

conditions.catalyst: mineral-assisted concentration; no unique catalyst required beyond surface setting
conditions.other: evaporitic or geothermal pool; anoxic; NH3 addition to activated nitrile

reactions.mechanism: Nucleophilic conjugate addition of ammonia to acrylonitrile gives beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies ammonia addition to acrylonitrile as the next step after Cu-coupling. Mineral agents are included to satisfy the required surface-mineral specification and to reflect plausible concentration on clays/sulfide surfaces in cyanosulfidic systems (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson & Sutherland, 2012).

reactions.name: Surface guanidinylation of beta-aminopropionitrile by cyanamide

conditions.other: neutral to mildly alkaline; incomplete conversion reported; cyanosulfidic network context

reactions.mechanism: The amine of beta-aminopropionitrile attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide to form a guanidinated nitrile derivative.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly states that beta-aminopropionitrile is treated with cyanamide to produce a guanidinated derivative, while also emphasizing that direct guanidinylation is relatively inefficient and leaves mixed material. That inefficiency is preserved here rather than hidden (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface photoredox reduction and cyanamide trapping to hemiaminal 37

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox with UV and H2S; mineral-supported irradiated surface
conditions.other: strong UV; oxygen-free; residual cyanamide traps beta-aminopropionaldehyde as stable hemiaminal

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction under Cu redox cycling and H2S generates the corresponding aldehyde equivalent, which is trapped extraordinarily efficiently by cyanamide to give stable hemiaminal 37.
reactions.reasoning: This step follows the report closely: UV-driven Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox chemistry in the presence of H2S leads to aldehyde generation and cyanamide trapping, with hemiaminal 37 identified as a key stabilizing intermediate. This trapping is essential because free aldehydes would otherwise be too reactive in complex mixtures (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface dark cyanohydrin formation from hemiaminal 37

conditions.other: dark reaction; anoxic; HCN addition in pooled solution

reactions.mechanism: HCN adds to the aldehydic/hemiaminal carbon center to generate a cyanohydrin intermediate in a Kiliani-Fischer-like homologation sequence.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly describes a dark reaction with HCN converting hemiaminal 37 into a cyanohydrin intermediate. Mineral agents are included as plausible concentrating surfaces in evaporitic or geothermal pools (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface sulfidolysis of cyanohydrin to alpha-hydroxythioamide
reactions.mechanism: Sulfide substitutes into the cyanohydrin-derived intermediate to produce an alpha-hydroxythioamide.
reactions.reasoning: This is directly stated in the report: addition of H2S to the cyanohydrin produces an alpha-hydroxythioamide. Sulfide minerals are explicitly appropriate for cyanide/sulfide surface chemistry (Patel et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2018).

reactions.name: Surface UV deoxygenation of alpha-hydroxythioamide to thioamide

conditions.catalyst: UV irradiation; Cu photoredox-compatible surface setting

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation drives deoxygenation of the alpha-hydroxythioamide to the corresponding thioamide.
reactions.reasoning: The report identifies UV irradiation of the alpha-hydroxythioamide as causing deoxygenation to yield a thioamide. This is a core part of the Patel et al. systems chemistry homologation sequence (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Surface photoreduction and final amination to arginine alpha-aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: UV with Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox in H2S-rich setting
conditions.other: oxygen-free; further photoreduction followed by HCN and NH3 addition

reactions.mechanism: Photoreduction of the thioamide followed by cyanide incorporation and amination forms the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The report explicitly concludes the cyanosulfidic sequence with further photoreduction of the thioamide followed by HCN and NH3 addition, producing the arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor. This is the most direct literature-supported prebiotic precursor to free arginine in the supplied research (Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolysis of arginine alpha-aminonitrile to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during geothermal cycling or standing pools; step is chemically expected but less directly documented than precursor formation

reactions.mechanism: Stepwise nitrile hydration and hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile into the corresponding amino acid.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is chemically standard and necessary to connect the literature-supported arginine alpha-aminonitrile to free arginine. The report emphasizes the precursor rather than this terminal conversion, so this node is included as a necessary but less directly specified completion step and should be regarded as modestly speculative in exact natural conditions (Strecker, 1850; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical assembly of ornithine-containing statistical peptides

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling or peptide aggregation; statistical peptide formation in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Concentration and condensation convert free ornithine into oligomeric ornithine-containing peptide pools.
reactions.reasoning: The report frames arginine emergence partly through a statistical protein era in which ornithine-containing polypeptides predate arginine-containing ones. This step is included as a connecting biochemical assembly process; exact free-ornithine origins remain uncertain and are therefore not overclaimed here (Longo et al., 2020; Forsythe et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical post-synthetic guanidination of ornithine peptide to arginine peptide

conditions.other: room temperature; neutral to slightly alkaline aqueous solution; statistical side-chain conversion

reactions.mechanism: The side-chain primary amine of ornithine residues attacks cyanamide, yielding guanidinated arginine residues within the peptide.
reactions.reasoning: This step is directly supported by the supplied report, which describes abiotic aqueous guanidination of ornithine side chains by cyanamide at room temperature and neutral to slightly alkaline pH, with marked improvement in foldability and nucleic-acid binding. This is the strongest non-canonical route to arginine in the research set (Longo et al., 2020).

reactions.name: Biochemical hydrolytic release of arginine from arginine-containing peptide

conditions.other: geothermal or pool hydrolysis; included as network-connecting release step rather than primary natural endpoint

reactions.mechanism: Partial peptide hydrolysis liberates free arginine from arginine-containing oligomers.
reactions.reasoning: The report's main evidence supports peptide guanidination, not necessarily quantitative release of free arginine. This hydrolytic release is therefore included as a necessary network-connecting step and is explicitly secondary to the better-supported peptide endpoint (Longo et al., 2020; Forsythe et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Biochemical ornithine to citrulline carbamoylation analogue

conditions.other: included only as a low-confidence protometabolic analogue; exact prebiotic carbamoyl donor unspecified in supplied report

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl transfer analogue converts side-chain amine of ornithine to ureido functionality giving citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: The report discusses citrulline as a biologically central intermediate and mentions formation from ornithine with carbamoyl phosphate in biology, but does not provide a chemically explicit prebiotic route under the supplied evidence. This reaction is therefore marked as speculative and low-confidence, included only to represent the reported protometabolic ornithine/citrulline/arginine conceptual connection (Bada & Lazcano, 2003; as discussed in the supplied review).

reactions.name: Biochemical citrulline conversion to arginine analogue

conditions.other: included only as a speculative protometabolic analogue to reflect literature discussion of urea-cycle-like interconversion

reactions.mechanism: Amidino-transfer/amination analogue converts citrulline to arginine in a conceptual protometabolic cycle.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied report discusses citrulline as a precursor/intermediate in biological arginine interconversion but does not provide a validated prebiotic chemistry for this step. It is therefore explicitly speculative and included only to reflect the review's mention of a possible protometabolic urea-cycle-like network; it should not be weighted as strongly as the cyanosulfidic or peptide-guanidination routes (as discussed in the supplied review; no direct prebiotic experimental paper specified).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine precursor route
pathways.description: Direct Patel/Sutherland-style route from HCN and acetylene through acrylonitrile, beta-aminopropionitrile, guanidinated intermediate, hemiaminal 37, cyanohydrin, alpha-hydroxythioamide, thioamide, and arginine alpha-aminonitrile to free arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with hydrothermal ammonia input
pathways.description: Same direct surface route, but explicitly fed by hydrothermal NH3 pool as the nitrogen source coupling vent chemistry to surface photoredox synthesis.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with hydrothermal H2S input
pathways.description: Direct surface route emphasizing hydrothermal sulfide delivery into the UV photoredox and sulfidolysis stages.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route emphasizing cyanamide trapping
pathways.description: Direct route highlighting cyanamide's dual role in guanidinylation and aldehyde trapping as hemiaminal 37.

pathways.name: Dark-plus-light homologation route to arginine
pathways.description: A traversal of the same core chemistry stressing alternation of dark cyanohydrin formation and UV photoredox reductions before hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal-to-surface integrated arginine route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction and reduced-nitrogen generation feed NH3 and H2S into the surface cyanosulfidic route, giving explicit environmental interconnection before final hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Peptide-first ornithine guanidination route
pathways.description: Secondary literature-supported route in which pre-existing ornithine-containing peptides are statistically guanidinated by cyanamide and later hydrolyzed to free arginine.

pathways.name: Surface cyanamide to peptide-guanylation route
pathways.description: A cross-environment route where surface-generated cyanamide is transferred into biochemical pools containing ornithine peptides, yielding arginine peptides and then arginine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal amino-acid pool to peptide modification route
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate/alanine chemistry contributes to a broader amino-acid pool and peptide assembly context, after which ornithine peptide guanidination provides arginine.

pathways.name: Speculative protometabolic ornithine-citrulline-arginine route
pathways.description: Low-confidence conceptual pathway included only because the review discusses citrulline and protometabolic urea-cycle-like interconversion; it should be weighted much lower than P1-P9.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Strong primary route and well aligned with the literature-supported Patel/Sutherland cyanosulfidic framework. The sequence from acrylonitrile through beta-aminopropionitrile, cyanamide guanidinylation, hemiaminal trapping, cyanohydrin formation, sulfidolysis, UV deoxygenation, and final alpha-aminonitrile formation is chemically coherent and matches the reported systems-chemistry logic. The only moderate uncertainty is the final hydrolysis from aminonitrile to free arginine, which is plausible but less directly established under fully prebiotic conditions than the precursor synthesis.
suggestions: Specify whether hydrolysis of the arginine alpha-aminonitrile occurs under neutral aqueous aging, geothermal cycling, or mineral-assisted conditions.
Include explicit Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox and UV details in the surface steps to strengthen environmental realism.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This route is acceptable as an integrated hydrothermal-to-surface narrative, but the claim that hydrothermal alanine chemistry meaningfully supplies ammonia for the surface arginine route is indirect. The main arginine chemistry remains strong; the hydrothermal insertion is more contextual than mechanistically necessary.
suggestions: Clarify how ammonia generated or concentrated in the hydrothermal branch is transferred into the surface cyanosulfidic setting.
Avoid overstating alanine formation as a direct precursor branch to arginine unless additional steps are provided.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good route if interpreted as vent-derived H2S contributing to a shallow photoredox setting. The surface branch remains literature-consistent. The hydrothermal connection is plausible geochemically but not strongly causal in the actual synthetic sequence.
suggestions: Make the transport and persistence of sulfide between hydrothermal and surface compartments explicit.
State that the hydrothermal branch supports reagent availability rather than direct arginine assembly.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Very strong pathway because it highlights the key literature feature: trapping of the reactive aldehyde as hemiaminal 37 by residual cyanamide. This addresses one of the major selectivity problems in prebiotic arginine synthesis and is one of the best-justified parts of the network.
suggestions: Emphasize that the efficiency of aldehyde trapping is a major reason this branch is prebiotically credible.
Add more explicit note that this route depends on sequential flow chemistry rather than a true one-pot system.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Coherent and faithful to the reported alternation of dark chemistry and UV photochemistry in the cyanosulfidic system. The dark cyanohydrin step followed by light-driven sulfur chemistry is especially consistent with the literature framing.
suggestions: Distinguish dark and irradiative phases more explicitly in pathway annotation.
Provide a note on likely pH window and anoxic conditions for preserving intermediates.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Good network-level interconnection pathway, but weaker as a strict synthetic route. Hydrothermal formate/pyruvate/alanine chemistry is literature-supported in general, yet its direct contribution to the arginine branch is not demonstrated. This pathway works better as an ecosystem feedstock integration scenario than as a linear synthesis route.
suggestions: Clarify that pyruvate and alanine are part of a connected prebiotic inventory, not necessary intermediates to arginine in this network.
Avoid implying a direct hydrothermal synthesis of arginine precursors beyond supported reduced-carbon supply.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: Reasonable secondary branch grounded in the literature on post-synthetic guanidination of ornithine residues by cyanamide. The main weakness is that peptide assembly from free ornithine is underspecified, and hydrolytic release of free arginine from statistical arginine-containing peptides is possible but not well constrained as a productive prebiotic route.
suggestions: Specify peptide condensation conditions and templates, such as wet-dry cycling, depsipeptide intermediates, or mineral-assisted oligomerization.
Treat peptide hydrolysis as a recycling/source process rather than a high-yield dedicated arginine production step.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: The cyanamide guanidination step itself is well supported for ornithine-containing peptides, but the pathway is too compressed to be fully persuasive as an arginine production route because the source of the ornithine peptide is not part of the route and the terminal hydrolysis remains weakly justified.
suggestions: Embed this as a branch within a longer peptide assembly and turnover cycle.
Explicitly state that this route is a systems-chemistry modification pathway, not a primary monomer-synthesis pathway.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is a conceptually interesting hydrothermal amino-acid pool to peptide-modification pathway, but it lacks a supported conversion from alanine-rich or general amino-acid pools to ornithine specifically. The peptide guanidination literature helps, yet the upstream ornithine availability gap lowers confidence.
suggestions: Add a literature-backed source of ornithine if this route is retained.
Do not rely on general amino acid abundance to imply ornithine availability without support.

pathway_evaluations.feedback: This is the weakest pathway. While the literature discusses citrulline and protometabolic interconversion conceptually, the specific prebiotic reactions shown here are not well supported as written. Ornithine carbamoylation usually requires a stronger carbamoyl donor than is specified, and conversion of citrulline to arginine in biology depends on ATP-driven argininosuccinate chemistry, not simple ammonia addition.
suggestions: Downgrade this branch explicitly to speculative status.
If retained, include a plausible carbamoyl donor and acknowledge the lack of direct experimental support for abiotic citrulline-to-arginine conversion.

evaluation.network_feedback: Overall this is a strong synthesis network centered on the best-supported prebiotic route to arginine: the cyanosulfidic synthesis of an arginine alpha-aminonitrile followed by hydrolysis. The network is topologically solid, connected, and shows good environmental interconnection without forcing unsupported hydrothermal arginine chemistry. It aligns well with the supplied literature by prioritizing the Cu-photoredox/UV/H2S/cyanamide sequence, correctly emphasizing aldehyde trapping via hemiaminal 37, and treating the ornithine-peptide guanidination branch as secondary. Major strengths are literature alignment, multiple convergent hubs, and a coherent surface-to-biochemical route to the target. Main weaknesses are that some hydrothermal nodes are only loosely connected to actual arginine production, the ornithine branch lacks a supported source of ornithine and overrelies on peptide hydrolysis as release chemistry, and the citrulline branch is largely speculative and not chemically persuasive in its current form. Also, because the submitted network summary omits full reaction metadata, mineral-agent compliance for every hydrothermal and surface step cannot be fully verified from the text alone, though the chemistry implies acceptable catalysts for the main literature-based route.
